text.title: Load Balancer Health Checks Failure Modes
text.summary: 3 antipatterns observed when wiring health checks. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Production incident: DB is down, but the LB still routes traffic; users see 500 errors while dashboards show "all backends healthy".
antipattern.root-cause: `/health` returns `{"status":"ok"}` unconditionally; it never touches the DB.
antipattern.fix: Add a `/health/ready` endpoint that runs `SELECT 1` on the primary DB + `PING` on the cache, bounded by 5 s timeouts. Return 503 if any check fails. Wire LB to `/health/ready`, not `/health`.

antipattern.symptom: Pod is killed mid-startup; restart loop. The DB migration that runs at boot takes 2 minutes; readiness fails during that window, and the same endpoint is also wired to liveness — kubelet restarts the pod.
antipattern.root-cause: Same handler (`/health`) wired to both `livenessProbe` and `readinessProbe`.
antipattern.fix: Split into `/health/live` (always 200 once process up) and `/health/ready` (deep probe). Wire `livenessProbe` to `/health/live` and `readinessProbe` to `/health/ready`. Add `startupProbe` for slow boots.

antipattern.symptom: Single hung DB connection makes the readiness probe hang for 30 s; the LB times out and removes the pod even though every other dependency is healthy.
antipattern.root-cause: Dependency calls inside the probe have no per-call timeout.
antipattern.fix: Wrap every dep call in a 1–5 s timeout (`context.WithTimeout` / `requests.get(..., timeout=3)`). The handler must explicitly return 503 on timeout, not let the LB's own probe timeout fire.
